full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Identifying eligible SVM papers from CORE A* venues (2009–2012) that satisfy methodological and reproducibility constraints
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Find me 10 SVM papers from 2009-2012 ICML/NeurIPS that are reproducible
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: This helped me generate an initial list of SVM-based papers from ICML and NeurIPS within the allowed year range. It saved time in scanning proceedings manually and gave me a starting shortlist aligned with the venue and year constraints.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I independently verified each shortlisted paper by checking the official ICML and NeurIPS proceedings and confirming publication year, authors, and venue classification.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Evaluating which shortlisted paper would allow meaningful explanation and experimental reproduction
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Search for research papers that strike a balance between conceptual depth and practical feasibility, where I can clearly justify my selection, explain the methodological contribution, and meaningfully analyze results. My preference is SVM-based methods.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: This helped narrow the options to papers that are not just applications of standard SVM, but introduce a modification or extension. It highlighted 'Learning Sparse SVM for Feature Selection on Very High Dimensional Data' as a strong candidate because it introduces sparsity into the SVM formulation, which directly connects to feature selection.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I read the abstract and method section of the paper to confirm that the sparsity-inducing formulation is a genuine methodological contribution rather than a benchmarking study.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Understanding the core idea of sparse SVM and its difference from standard SVM
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Explain how sparse SVM differs from standard linear SVM. How does L1 regularization affect feature selection and model behavior?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: The explanation clarified that introducing an L1 penalty on the weight vector encourages many coefficients to become exactly zero. This directly links SVM classification with embedded feature selection, which is central to the paper’s contribution.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I confirmed this by reviewing the optimization objective in the paper and comparing it with the standard soft-margin SVM formulation covered in Unit 2 lectures.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Checking reproducibility and CPU feasibility for Part B planning
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Can the sparse SVM paper (ICML 2010) be reproduced on a CPU-only laptop using scikit-learn? What would a simplified reproduction look like?
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: This interaction confirmed that a simplified version of sparse SVM can be implemented using LinearSVC with L1 penalty in scikit-learn. It suggested comparing standard L2 SVM versus L1-regularized SVM on high-dimensional datasets to observe sparsity effects.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I checked sklearn documentation and verified that LinearSVC supports L1 regularization with dual=False. I also confirmed that publicly available datasets (e.g., UCI datasets) are sufficient for reproduction.

full_llm_interaction_log.purpose: Ensuring the JSON disclosure strictly follows the required Part A schema
full_llm_interaction_log.prompt: Rewrite my JSON so that it strictly follows the required structure and key naming conventions from the Part A instructions.
full_llm_interaction_log.how_it_helped: This ensured that all required sections (student_metadata, llm_tools_used, full_llm_interaction_log, top_5_prompts, student_declaration) are present and correctly formatted according to the official specification.
full_llm_interaction_log.student_verification: I rechecked the final JSON against the Part A instructions to confirm that all required fields are present and no key names are missing or incorrectly formatted.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Can the sparse SVM paper (ICML 2010) be reproduced on a CPU-only laptop using scikit-learn? What would a simplified reproduction look like?
top_5_prompts.why_important: CPU-only reproducibility is explicitly required in the rubric. This prompt confirmed feasibility and gave me a clear experimental direction for Part B, directly impacting the reproducibility evaluation criteria.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Explain how sparse SVM differs from standard linear SVM. How does L1 regularization affect feature selection and model behavior?
top_5_prompts.why_important: This helped me clearly understand the core contribution of the paper — introducing sparsity into SVM. Without understanding this difference, I would not be able to properly explain the paper in Part C.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Search for research papers that strike a balance between conceptual depth and practical feasibility, where I can clearly justify my selection, explain the methodological contribution, and meaningfully analyze results. My preference is SVM-based methods.
top_5_prompts.why_important: This prompt shaped the selection decision by ensuring that the chosen paper has both theoretical substance and practical feasibility, rather than being purely application-driven.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Find me 10 SVM papers from 2009-2012 ICML/NeurIPS that are reproducible
top_5_prompts.why_important: This was the starting point that ensured all candidate papers satisfied the strict venue and year constraints before deeper evaluation.

top_5_prompts.prompt: Rewrite my JSON so that it strictly follows the required structure and key naming conventions from the Part A instructions.
top_5_prompts.why_important: This ensured that the submission complies fully with the required disclosure format and avoids technical penalties related to schema or structure.

student_declaration.statement: I declare that this JSON file contains a complete and honest record of my LLM usage for Part A.
